impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products allegedly lack substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under antitrust law. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent, defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF benefit from protection against patent holders leveraging patents to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when tying unpatented product purchases to patent licenses, even where the products can be used in patented systems, if they also have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by emulating a program and comparing function calls to a model are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether damages may be recovered for foreign sales of infringing software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract under Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also ruled that damages based on Norton's foreign sales were improperly awarded. The decision also required reconsideration of enhanced damages and attorneys' fees due to reversal of a related contempt order.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from stronger § 101 eligibility scrutiny of software patents, and the foreign-sales damages ruling limits patent holders' ability to recover damages for overseas revenue.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders, particularly universities and research institutions asserting cybersecurity and software patents, face heightened risk of invalidation under § 101 and reduced damages for products sold globally.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities and delivering the content for rendering are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing a jury verdict finding Netflix infringed the '715 patent and awarding $2.5 million in damages. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities. The decision required entry of judgment for Netflix across all patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering conventional content-delivery and adaptive streaming concepts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming media and content delivery space face increased risk that their claims will be found abstract under § 101, even after surviving district court challenges.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution where the patentee acquired prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) describing similar technology, and whether summary judgment was proper on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (granted against Tenaris) and Walker Process fraud (granted against Global Tubing), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The court held that questions about Tenaris's intent to deceive the PTO and whether its patent enforcement constituted attempted monopolization required trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to send inequitable conduct claims to trial where there is evidence of withheld prior art, creating leverage in patent disputes.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire companies with prior art face significant litigation risk if they fail to disclose that art during prosecution, and may face antitrust exposure under the Walker Process doctrine.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket confidentiality practice for questionnaire responses is incompatible with the governing statute and does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The court denied the ITC's mandamus petition but treated it as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic competitors gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, potentially improving their ability to challenge antidumping and countervailing duty determinations with access to previously sealed information.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit business data in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of public disclosure of competitively sensitive information, potentially chilling candid submissions in trade investigations.

impacts.caseName: Genuine Enabling Technology LLC v. Sony Group Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's failure to account for all elements of a disclosed corresponding structure in a means-plus-function infringement analysis is fatal to its infringement case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of noninfringement for Sony, holding that GET failed to adequately map all elements of the disclosed corresponding structure for a means-plus-function 'encoding means' limitation to Sony's PlayStation controllers and consoles. The court emphasized that patentees must account for all components of the identified corresponding structure.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against means-plus-function patent claims benefit from strict requirements that patentees must map every element of the corresponding structure to accused products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting means-plus-function claims face heightened burden to thoroughly analyze and map all disclosed structural elements, risking summary judgment if their expert analysis is incomplete.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft on network virtualization patents. The court found the district court applied too stringent a standard in excluding the damages testimony, and that the resulting summary judgment based solely on absence of a provable remedy was improper.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more permissive standard for admitting damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that technical damages methodologies will be excluded at the Daubert stage.
impacts.negativeImplications: Large technology companies like Microsoft face increased exposure as patent plaintiffs gain a clearer path to presenting damages evidence at trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement and granting JMOL to the defendant in a medical device patent case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two experts and JMOL for DePuy in a case involving spinal surgery derotation tool patents. The court found the district court improperly excluded infringement testimony and erroneously granted judgment as a matter of law, remanding for a new trial where both experts may testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders, particularly individual inventors, benefit from the court's protective approach to expert testimony admissibility in complex medical device patent cases.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies defending patent cases face increased trial exposure when appellate courts reverse expert exclusions, requiring them to contest infringement on the merits.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute legislative rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions on IPR institution discretion are 'general statements of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution decisions are insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad, flexible discretion to manage IPR institution policies without the burden of formal rulemaking, benefiting patent holders who seek fewer IPR challenges to their patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies like Apple that rely on IPR as a cost-effective mechanism to challenge patents face continued uncertainty and limited judicial recourse when the PTO declines to institute review proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered a limited exclusion order rather than a general exclusion order against defaulting respondents in a Section 337 trademark investigation, and whether an appeal of the no-violation finding as to active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's untimely appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents but affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The court upheld the ITC's statutory presumption under § 1337(g)(1) that facts alleged in the complaint are true when respondents default, while declining to issue a broader general exclusion order.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners benefit from the ITC's mandatory presumption of truth for default respondents, ensuring some enforcement remedy even when active respondents prevail on the merits.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark holders seeking broad general exclusion orders face difficulty obtaining them when the ITC finds no violation as to participating respondents, limiting enforcement against non-defaulting importers.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether eligible restaurant applicants have Tucker Act jurisdiction to seek damages for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants where the SBA failed to award grants in the statutory order of application receipt.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss claims by over 300 restaurants seeking unpaid RRF grants. The court held that the statute's mandatory language requiring SBA to award grants in application order created a money-mandating obligation sufficient for Tucker Act jurisdiction, allowing businesses to pursue damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that applied for but did not receive pandemic relief grants may pursue monetary claims against the government where a statute mandates payment in a specific order.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 are entitled to overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday pay enhancements available under Title 38 provisions that IHS has elected to apply for base pay purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced compensation for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 provisions. The court held that IHS nurses who receive Title 38 base pay rates may also be entitled to the corresponding enhanced pay rates for non-standard work hours, remanding for further proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential back-pay liability for nurses and other healthcare workers who were paid base rates under Title 38 but denied corresponding enhanced compensation for overtime, nighttime, and weekend work.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims containing means-plus-function 'control means' limitations are indefinite where the specification describes the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's conclusion that asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were indefinite. The court held that the specification's description of the 'control means' corresponding structure—including a controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system—was sufficiently definite despite being described as 'conventional,' remanding for further infringement proceedings against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's rejection of a strict indefiniteness standard for means-plus-function claims where the specification identifies corresponding structural components, even if described as conventional.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies like Deere face renewed infringement exposure when indefiniteness defenses are rejected on appeal, requiring continued litigation on the merits.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the jury's verdict of nonobviousness and damages award were supported by substantial evidence in a case involving pre-lit artificial tree patents with integrated electrical and mechanical connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages in a case where Willis asserted patents on pre-lit artificial trees with simultaneous mechanical-electrical trunk connections. The court upheld the jury's findings on nonobviousness and the damages award against Polygroup's Quick Set tree products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on nonobviousness and damages, reinforcing the high bar for overturning favorable verdicts on JMOL.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors face continued enforcement risk when jury verdicts on validity and damages are upheld on appeal, limiting post-trial avenues to challenge unfavorable outcomes.
